proposals.hypothesis: Higher habitual intake of dietary kaempferol is associated with lower circulating levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α, CRP) and a reduced incidence of specific inflammation-related cancers (e.g., colorectal, breast) in a dose-dependent manner.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective analysis within an existing large cohort (e.g., UK Biobank or EPIC). Kaempferol intake will be quantified by linking repeated food-frequency questionnaires to an expanded polyphenol-specific database (e.g., Phenol-Explorer). Cancer incidence will be tracked via national registries. We will measure baseline inflammation markers in a nested case-control subset. Statistical analysis will employ multivariable Cox proportional hazards models for cancer risk and multiple linear regression for inflammation markers, adjusting for key confounders including other dietary factors, BMI, and smoking.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, population-level evidence for the long-term health effects of kaempferol, moving beyond mechanistic and animal studies. It will quantify the potential protective effect size and identify threshold intake levels, offering concrete data for dietary guidelines. The integrated analysis of intake, inflammation, and disease incidence will elucidate a key potential pathway.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies link general flavonoid intake to health, this proposal focuses specifically on kaempferol, a compound Dr. Daglia has extensively studied, using a quantified intake approach rather than food groups. It uniquely integrates detailed exposure assessment with hard clinical endpoints and intermediate inflammatory biomarkers in a single large cohort.

proposals.title: Rational Design and Synthesis of Kaempferol Mimetics: Non-Flavonoid Scaffolds with Enhanced Pro-Oxidant Switch and Drug-Like Properties
proposals.motivation: Kaempferol, a dietary flavonoid, exhibits a well-documented 'pro-oxidant switch' that can induce selective cytotoxicity in cancer cells, but its poor bioavailability and rapid metabolism limit therapeutic application. Pre-2026 research highlights the need to translate the beneficial bioactivity of polyphenols into compounds with improved pharmacokinetic profiles. This project addresses the inverse problem of designing novel chemical entities that mimic this key bioactivity while circumventing the inherent limitations of the flavonoid scaffold.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by using computational pharmacophore modeling based on kaempferol's redox-active structure, we can design and synthesize novel non-flavonoid compounds that retain or enhance the pro-oxidant switch mechanism while exhibiting superior drug-like properties, including metabolic stability and membrane permeability.
proposals.method: We will employ a structure-based design approach. First, a pharmacophore model of kaempferol's pro-oxidant activity will be generated using molecular docking and dynamics simulations (AutoDock Vina, GROMACS) to identify essential features like the phenolic OH arrangement and redox potential. This model will guide the virtual screening of non-flavonoid chemical libraries (e.g., ZINC15) and the de novo design of candidate scaffolds. Selected virtual hits will be synthesized, focusing on simplified, non-flavonoid cores (e.g., chalcone-like or phenolic acid derivatives). Their pro-oxidant capacity, antioxidant-to-pro-oxidant switch (measured by ROS generation in cell-free and cellular assays under varying conditions), and key ADMET properties (e.g., metabolic stability in liver microsomes, Caco-2 permeability) will be experimentally validated.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first rationally designed, synthetic non-flavonoid mimetics of kaempferol's pro-oxidant switch, providing novel chemical tools to study redox-based therapies. It will establish a design framework for translating the bioactivity of complex dietary polyphenols into drug-like leads, directly contributing to the fields of medicinal chemistry and nutraceutical development.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the 'inverse design' approach, moving from a natural product's complex bioactivity to a simplified, optimized synthetic mimic, rather than merely modifying the natural scaffold. This focuses explicitly on capturing the context-dependent pro-oxidant switch, a nuanced mechanism rarely targeted in mimetic design pre-2026.

proposals.title: Engineering Saccharomyces cerevisiae for the De Novo Biosynthesis of Novel, Bioactive Polyphenol-Saponin Hybrid Molecules
proposals.motivation: Plant-derived polyphenols and saponins, such as kaempferol derivatives, show significant neuroprotective, antioxidant, and hypoglycemic potential, as evidenced by Dr. Daglia's prior work. However, their natural extraction is limited by low yields, structural complexity, and the inability to access 'non-natural' analogs with potentially superior bioactivity and pharmacokinetics, such as enhanced absorption or gut microbiota interactions. Microbial biosynthesis offers a sustainable and tunable production platform but has not been systematically applied to create entirely novel polyphenol scaffolds.
proposals.hypothesis: By combining key biosynthetic enzymes from polyphenol and triterpenoid saponin pathways in a single engineered yeast chassis, we can produce novel structural hybrids that do not exist in nature, which will exhibit enhanced or novel bioactive properties relevant to metabolic and cognitive health.
proposals.method: We will engineer Saccharomyces cerevisiae to express a heterologous pathway combining a tyrosine ammonia-lyase (TAL) for p-coumaric acid production with a chalcone synthase (CHS) and specific cytochrome P450s (e.g., flavonoid 3'-hydroxylase) to generate a core flavonoid scaffold. Concurrently, we will integrate a β-amyrin synthase (from the triterpenoid saponin pathway) and glycosyltransferases. Using structure-guided enzyme engineering (via Rosetta-based computational design) on key tailoring enzymes (e.g., O-methyltransferases, glycosyltransferases), we will diversify the core structures. The resulting novel compounds will be purified and screened for antioxidant (ORAC assay), anti-acetylcholinesterase activity, and hypoglycemic potential (α-glucosidase inhibition), followed by in vivo evaluation in a scopolamine-induced amnesia model.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept microbial platform for generating 'non-natural' polyphenolic compounds, moving beyond mere replication of plant metabolites. It will provide a new library of bioactive hybrid molecules and establish structure-activity relationships for engineered polyphenols, directly contributing to the fields of nutraceutical discovery and synthetic biology for natural product analogs.
proposals.novelty: While microbial production of individual plant polyphenols is known, the deliberate design and biosynthesis of novel polyphenol-saponin hybrid structures through combinatorial pathway engineering and computational enzyme design represents a significant and unexplored frontier in accessing optimized bioactives.

proposals.title: Structural Determinants of Bioactivity in Polyphenol-Saponin Complexes: Decoupling Antioxidant from Antimicrobial Mechanisms via Targeted Chemical Modulation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on plant-derived bioactive compounds like polyphenols and saponins often reports strong correlations between antioxidant capacity and other biological activities, such as antimicrobial effects. This correlation complicates the identification of the precise structural features responsible for each specific activity, hindering the rational design of molecules with targeted therapeutic applications. A systematic effort to decouple these mechanisms is crucial for developing more effective and specific anti-infective or neuroprotective agents without relying on generalized redox activity.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific structural modifications to polyphenol-saponin complexes, particularly targeting hydroxyl group positioning and glycosylation patterns, will selectively diminish antioxidant capacity while preserving or enhancing intrinsic antimicrobial activity, indicating separable mechanisms of action.
proposals.method: We will employ a targeted phytochemical approach using Desmodium elegans and Dictyota dichotoma extracts, rich in kaempferol derivatives and saponins, as starting material. Key polyphenol structures will be selectively acetylated or methylated to block redox-active hydroxyl groups. Purified native and derivatized compounds will be profiled in parallel using standardized assays: antioxidant capacity (ORAC, FRAP) and antimicrobial activity (MIC against Gram-positive/negative bacteria and fungi). Multivariate statistical analysis (Principal Component Analysis and Partial Least Squares Regression) will be used to correlate specific structural features from LC-MS/MS and NMR data with bioactivity profiles, identifying moieties essential for each function.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clear map of the chemical features in polyphenol-saponin systems that drive antimicrobial action independently of their antioxidant potential. It will deliver a set of strategically modified lead compounds with focused bioactivity. The findings will advance the field of natural product drug discovery by offering a rational methodology to 'tune' complex plant metabolites for specific biological endpoints.
proposals.novelty: While many studies describe the bioactivity of plant extracts, this proposal moves beyond correlation to actively engineer separation of mechanisms through targeted chemical intervention. The integrated use of selective derivatization with multivariate analysis to decouple intertwined bioactivities in polyphenol-saponin complexes represents a novel, hypothesis-driven approach in phytochemistry.

proposals.title: Synergistic Antibacterial Effects of Polyphenol-Enriched Plant Extracts with Conventional Antibiotics: Mechanistic Insights from Gut Microbiota Interactions and Biofilm Disruption
proposals.motivation: The rise of antibiotic-resistant pathogens is a critical global health threat pre-2026, necessitating novel strategies to enhance existing antibiotic efficacy. Polyphenols, known for their antimicrobial and anti-biofilm properties, and their complex interactions with gut microbiota and metabolism, offer a promising but underexplored avenue for combination therapy to combat resistant infections.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific polyphenol combinations, particularly those rich in kaempferol and saponins, will act synergistically with conventional antibiotics (e.g., beta-lactams, fluoroquinolones) by disrupting bacterial membranes and biofilms, thereby significantly lowering the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of the antibiotics.
proposals.method: We will extract and characterize polyphenol-rich fractions from *Desmodium elegans* and *Dictyota dichotoma*, focusing on kaempferol and saponin content. Synergy will be tested using checkerboard microdilution assays against clinically relevant resistant bacteria (e.g., MRSA, *E. coli* ESBL). Mechanistic studies will include biofilm biomass quantification (crystal violet assay), membrane permeability assays (propidium iodide uptake), and in silico molecular docking to predict interactions of key polyphenols with bacterial resistance proteins (e.g., beta-lactamases).
proposals.contribution: This work will identify specific, natural compound-antibiotic pairings with validated synergistic potential, providing a data-driven strategy to combat antibiotic resistance. It will also elucidate the dual mechanisms of action, linking direct antimicrobial effects with potential modulation of bacterial virulence and persistence through biofilm disruption.
proposals.novelty: While polyphenol-antibiotic synergy is known, this proposal uniquely integrates Dr. Daglia's expertise in saponin/polyphenol metabolism and gut interactions with a direct focus on overcoming specific resistance mechanisms, moving beyond simple additive effects to define mechanistically informed combinations.

proposals.title: Rational Design of Polyphenol-Saponin Hybrid Derivatives for Dual Antimicrobial and Anti-inflammatory Activity: Synthesis, In Vitro Evaluation, and Mechanistic Insights
proposals.motivation: Chronic infections are often exacerbated by dysregulated host inflammatory responses, leading to tissue damage. Pre-2026 research by Maria Daglia and others highlights the individual antimicrobial properties of polyphenols (e.g., kaempferol) and the immunomodulatory/absorption-enhancing effects of saponins. However, single-target agents are insufficient for managing complex infection-inflammation cycles. There is a critical need for multi-target therapeutic strategies that directly combat pathogens while modulating the host's inflammatory cascade.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chemically engineered hybrid molecules, conjugating specific antimicrobial polyphenol scaffolds with bioenhancing saponin moieties, will exhibit synergistic dual functionality: enhanced direct antimicrobial activity and potent suppression of pro-inflammatory signaling pathways (e.g., NF-κB).
proposals.method: We will employ a structure-based design approach, using molecular docking (AutoDock Vina) to model interactions of polyphenol cores (from Desmodium elegans and Dictyota dichotoma) with bacterial targets (e.g., microbial enzymes) and the saponin moiety with membrane models. Promising virtual hybrids will be synthesized via ester or glycosidic linkages. Their dual efficacy will be tested in vitro: antimicrobial potential against resistant bacterial strains (MIC/MBC assays) and anti-inflammatory activity in LPS-stimulated macrophages (measuring TNF-α, IL-6, and NF-κB nuclear translocation). Absorption and gut microbiome interaction potential will be assessed using simulated digestion and Caco-2 cell models, informed by Daglia's prior work on saponin mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel library of characterized polyphenol-saponin hybrids with validated dual bioactivity. It will provide mechanistic insights into how structural conjugation influences both antimicrobial potency and host-cell immunomodulation. The project will establish a rational design framework for multi-functional natural product derivatives, moving beyond simple extracts to targeted molecular entities.
proposals.novelty: While the individual bioactivities of polyphenols and saponins are known, the intentional design of covalent hybrids to leverage and potentially synergize these distinct properties (direct antimicrobial action and host-directed anti-inflammation) is a novel strategy. This approach integrates Daglia's expertise in saponin mechanisms and polyphenol profiling into a unified chemical biology effort.

proposals.title: A Multi-Omics and Gut Microbiota Analysis of Saponin-Rich Plant Extracts to Deconvolute Discrepancies Between Preclinical Neuroprotective Efficacy and Human Bioactivity
proposals.motivation: The translation of promising neuroprotective effects from in vitro and animal models to human health outcomes remains a major bottleneck in nutritional neuroscience. Pre-2026, while compounds like saponins and polyphenols show strong anti-amyloid, antioxidant, and anti-acetylcholinesterase activity in models, their human bioavailability, metabolism, and interaction with the gut microbiome are poorly characterized, leading to failed clinical expectations. This gap critically undermines the development of effective dietary interventions and nutraceuticals for cognitive decline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the discrepancy between preclinical neuroprotective activity and human efficacy of saponin-rich plant extracts is primarily governed by inter-species differences in gut microbiota-mediated metabolism and subsequent systemic bioavailability of active metabolites.
proposals.method: We will employ a comparative multi-omics pipeline. First, standardized extracts from *Desmodium elegans* (rich in kaempferol glycosides and saponins) will be characterized via LC-MS/MS. Their neuroprotective properties (acetylcholinesterase inhibition, anti-aggregation) will be confirmed in murine primary neuron cultures and a scopolamine-induced amnesia mouse model. Parallelly, in vitro human gut microbiome models (SHIME) inoculated with donor feces will be used to simulate microbial metabolism of the extracts. Metabolomic profiling (UPLC-QTOF-MS) of bio-transformed compounds will be compared to metabolites detected in mouse plasma and brain tissue. Finally, key microbial taxa driving metabolic conversion will be identified via 16S rRNA sequencing and metagenomic analysis of the SHIME communities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic framework linking specific gut microbial transformations to the bioavailability of neuroactive metabolites from saponin-rich botanicals. It will yield validated microbial biomarkers predictive of extract metabolism and identify which preclinical activity assays (e.g., anti-acetylcholinesterase) are most relevant to human physiology. This directly informs the design of more predictive preclinical models and personalized nutrition strategies.
proposals.novelty: While gut metabolism of polyphenols is studied, a systematic, multi-omics comparison of saponin metabolism between controlled animal models and a simulated human gut ecosystem, explicitly to validate preclinical activity endpoints, is novel. This approach moves beyond simple correlation to establish causal microbial pathways affecting translational relevance.

proposals.title: Investigating Auxiliary Biosynthetic Routes for Essential Oil Constituents in Medicinal Plants: A Multi-Omics and In Silico Approach
proposals.motivation: Essential oils are primarily biosynthesized via the mevalonate (MVA) and methylerythritol phosphate (MEP) isoprenoid pathways. However, pre-2026 research on plant secondary metabolism suggests significant cross-talk between pathways and potential alternative origins for certain volatile compounds. This gap limits our understanding of metabolic plasticity and our ability to engineer or optimize the production of bioactive volatiles with health benefits, such as those studied in Dr. Daglia's work on polyphenols and saponins.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific essential oil constituents, particularly phenylpropanoid-related volatiles and degraded carotenoids, are synthesized via auxiliary or hybrid biosynthetic routes that integrate precursors from the shikimate or carotenoid pathways alongside canonical isoprenoid building blocks.
proposals.method: We will employ a targeted multi-omics approach on model medicinal plants (e.g., *Desmodium elegans*). Metabolic profiling (GC-MS, LC-MS) will identify essential oil constituents and potential pathway intermediates under controlled stress conditions. Concurrent transcriptomics (RNA-seq) will map co-expression networks of genes from isoprenoid, shikimate, and carotenoid pathways. Key enzyme candidates (e.g., novel terpene synthases, cleavage dioxygenases) will be validated via heterologous expression in *E. coli* and *in vitro* assays. *In silico* molecular docking and dynamics simulations (using AutoDock Vina and GROMACS) will model enzyme-substrate interactions for non-canonical precursors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated map of potential auxiliary routes for volatile biosynthesis in medicinal plants, directly linking transcriptomic data to metabolic output. It will expand the fundamental framework of plant secondary metabolism and identify novel enzymatic targets for biotechnological manipulation to enhance the yield of specific bioactive volatiles.
proposals.novelty: While the core isoprenoid pathways are well-defined, systematically searching for and validating auxiliary biosynthetic origins for essential oil compounds using a multi-omics pipeline combined with *in silico* structural biology represents a novel, non-obvious integration of Dr. Daglia's expertise in phytochemistry and bioactivity evaluation with modern biosynthetic inquiry.

proposals.title: Synergistic and Antagonistic Interactions in Whole Essential Oils: A Multi-Methodological Approach Integrating Chemical Profiling, In Vitro Bioassays, and In Silico Modeling
proposals.motivation: Essential oils (EOs) are complex mixtures of terpenes and phenolics with documented biological activities, but their pharmacological effects are often attributed to single major components, overlooking the potential interactive effects of the whole phytocomplex. Understanding the synergy or antagonism among EO constituents is crucial for rationalizing their traditional uses and for developing more effective and standardized phytopharmaceuticals, moving beyond reductionist approaches prevalent in pre-2026 research.
proposals.hypothesis: The biological activity of a whole essential oil is not merely the sum of its individual major components, but is significantly modulated by synergistic or antagonistic interactions among its minor and major constituents.
proposals.method: We will select three EOs with known anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties (e.g., from oregano, thyme, and clove). Chemical profiling will be performed via GC-MS. Key individual compounds (e.g., carvacrol, thymol, eugenol) and reconstituted mixtures (both full-profile and simplified) will be tested in parallel with the whole EO in relevant in vitro assays (e.g., LPS-induced NO production in macrophages, antimicrobial MIC/MBC against S. aureus and E. coli). Interaction analysis will employ the Combination Index (CI) method via CompuSyn software. In silico molecular docking and network pharmacology approaches will be used to predict multi-target interactions and potential mechanisms underlying observed synergies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust methodological framework for the systematic study of interactions within complex botanical extracts. It will generate concrete data on specific synergistic pairs or fractions within EOs, offering a scientific basis for the superior efficacy of whole extracts over isolated compounds and informing better standardization strategies for herbal products.
proposals.novelty: While synergy studies for defined compound pairs exist, this proposal integrates chemical reconstruction, rigorous bioassay, and computational modeling to deconvolute interactions within the intact, chemically defined whole essential oil, a level of systematic analysis not commonly applied to EOs in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Integrating Physiologically-Based Pharmacokinetic (PBPK) Modeling and Gut Microbiota Interaction Analysis to Predict the Bioavailability and Neuroactive Effects of Polyphenol-Rich Essential Oil Components
proposals.motivation: Essential oils and plant extracts are rich in bioactive compounds like polyphenols and saponins, but their therapeutic potential is limited by unpredictable absorption, metabolism, and distribution. Pre-2026, advanced pharmacokinetic (PK) and pharmacodynamic (PD) modeling is standard for synthetic drugs but rarely applied to complex natural mixtures, creating a critical gap in translating in vitro bioactivity (e.g., neuroprotection, hypoglycemia) to reliable in vivo outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting PBPK/PD modeling frameworks, coupled with in vitro absorption and gut microbiota metabolism data, can accurately predict the systemic exposure and neuropharmacodynamic effects of key components in polyphenol-enriched essential oils.
proposals.method: We will select a model essential oil (e.g., from Desmodium elegans or related species) and characterize its major components (e.g., kaempferol derivatives, saponins). In vitro parameters (permeability, metabolic stability) will be generated using Caco-2 and hepatocyte models. Gut microbiota-mediated metabolism of saponins will be assessed in fecal batch cultures. These data will inform the development of a whole-body PBPK model using established software (e.g., GastroPlus or PK-Sim). The model will be linked to a PD component targeting scopolamine-induced cholinergic dysfunction, validated against in vivo pharmacokinetic and cognitive performance data from a rodent model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic computational framework to predict the in vivo fate and efficacy of complex natural products. It will directly contribute to the rational development of standardized, evidence-based botanical therapeutics, particularly for neurological conditions, by bridging the gap between traditional phytochemistry and modern drug development paradigms.
proposals.novelty: While PBPK modeling is established for synthetics, its integration with targeted in vitro gut microbiota interaction studies and application to the multi-component, metabolically labile space of essential oils and polyphenol extracts represents a significant and non-obvious methodological advance beyond standard chemical profiling and in vivo screening.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic Profiling Reveals Heterogeneous Cellular Responses to Tea Tree (Melaleuca alternifolia) Essential Oil in Human Keratinocytes and Macrophages
proposals.motivation: Essential oils (EOs) are complex mixtures of terpenes and phenolics with documented antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, but their effects are typically measured in bulk cell populations, masking potential cell-to-cell variability. This heterogeneity is critical to understand for precision applications in dermatology and immunomodulation, as differential responses could explain efficacy and toxicity profiles. Pre-2026 single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) technology now allows for the dissection of these nuanced, individual cellular reactions to natural product exposures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to tea tree essential oil will induce distinct, heterogeneous transcriptional programs in individual human keratinocytes and macrophages, revealing subpopulations with differential sensitivity linked to specific metabolic and inflammatory pathways.
proposals.method: We will treat primary human keratinocyte and macrophage co-cultures with a sub-cytotoxic dose of characterized Melaleuca alternifolia oil. Single-cell suspensions will be processed using the 10x Genomics Chromium platform for scRNA-seq. Bioinformatic analysis will utilize Seurat (v4) for clustering, differential expression (Wilcoxon rank-sum test), and trajectory inference (Monocle3). We will integrate these findings with targeted LC-MS analysis of key oil components (e.g., terpinen-4-ol) and correlate cellular subtypes with pre-2026 knowledge on kaempferol and saponin pathways from Dr. Daglia's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution map of single-cell transcriptional responses to a clinically relevant essential oil, identifying resistant and sensitive cellular subtypes. It will mechanistically link specific oil components to variable immune and barrier function outcomes, advancing beyond bulk 'anti-' activity descriptions to a precision understanding of EO bioactivity.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Daglia's prior work studied bulk effects of polyphenol and saponin extracts, applying single-cell omics to the complex phytochemical milieu of an essential oil is novel. This approach moves from assessing average 'effects' to defining the spectrum of individual cellular responses, a scale of analysis not yet applied to EOs in the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Beyond Bioavailability: Investigating the Role of Microbial Metabolism and Receptor Affinity in Limiting the Neuroprotective Efficacy of Polyphenol-Rich Extracts
proposals.motivation: Despite promising in vitro bioactivity, many polyphenol-based interventions, including those for neurodegenerative diseases, show inconsistent clinical efficacy. The predominant explanation has been low systemic bioavailability. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests that even when absorbed, polyphenols undergo extensive microbial and phase II metabolism, generating metabolites with potentially altered activity. Furthermore, their interactions with key neuronal receptors (e.g., cholinergic, NMDA) may be weak or indirect, limiting their functional impact in the brain.
proposals.hypothesis: The clinical efficacy of neuroprotective polyphenol extracts is limited not primarily by low bioavailability, but by (1) the generation of less active microbial metabolites and (2) insufficient affinity or specificity for critical neuronal receptors involved in memory and neuroinflammation.
proposals.method: Using a multi-target approach, we will first characterize the microbial metabolites of a standardized Desmodium elegans DC. polyphenol extract via in vitro fecal fermentation coupled with UHPLC-QTOF-MS. The parent compounds and their major microbial metabolites will then be screened for binding affinity to human acetylcholinesterase, NMDA, and GABAA receptors using molecular docking (AutoDock Vina) and surface plasmon resonance. Finally, the most promising compounds will be tested in a scopolamine-induced amnesia mouse model, comparing cognitive performance (Morris water maze) and brain receptor expression (western blot) between groups administered the parent extract versus its key microbial metabolite.
proposals.contribution: This work will shift the narrative from 'bioavailability' to 'bioactivity of metabolites,' identifying specific metabolic transformations that diminish efficacy. It will provide a mechanistic framework to explain clinical variability and guide the development of next-generation polyphenol formulations, such as prebiotic combinations or metabolite-based nutraceuticals, for neurodegenerative disease prevention.
proposals.novelty: While bioavailability is well-studied, this proposal uniquely integrates gut microbial metabolism profiling with high-throughput receptor screening and in vivo validation to directly test whether metabolic fate and target engagement are more critical bottlenecks than absorption for polyphenol efficacy.

proposals.title: Beyond Targeted Delivery: Evaluating the Systemic Trade-offs of Isolated Polyphenol Fractions Versus Complex Plant Extracts in Neuroprotection and Gut Microbiota Modulation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research strongly focuses on enhancing the bioavailability and targeted delivery of specific polyphenols (e.g., kaempferol) for therapeutic benefits. However, this reductionist approach may overlook the synergistic, broad-spectrum protective roles of complex polyphenol mixtures found in whole plant extracts, which can modulate multiple pathways and interact with the gut microbiota. Understanding the potential drawbacks of over-targeting is crucial for developing more effective nutraceutical and therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while targeted delivery of isolated polyphenol fractions improves specific pharmacokinetic parameters, it compromises the broader systemic protective effects—including multi-target neuroprotection and prebiotic gut microbiota modulation—observed with complex, polyphenol-rich whole plant extracts.
proposals.method: We will use a comparative in vivo study in a scopolamine-induced amnesia mouse model (Alzheimer's disease model). Animals will be treated with: 1) a standardized, polyphenol-enriched extract of *Desmodium elegans* (as used in Daglia's prior work), 2) a purified, targeted fraction of its dominant polyphenols (e.g., kaempferol glycosides), and 3) a vehicle control. Behavioral tests (Morris water maze) will assess cognitive function. Systemic effects will be evaluated via gut microbiota profiling (16S rRNA sequencing), measurement of systemic antioxidant status (SOD, CAT, GSH), and inflammatory markers (IL-6, TNF-α) in brain and plasma. In silico network pharmacology analysis will map the multi-target potential of the complex extract versus the isolated fraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct experimental evidence of the functional trade-offs between targeted delivery and holistic bioactivity of polyphenols. It will contribute a critical, systems-level perspective to nutraceutical science, informing whether future development should prioritize isolated compounds or standardized complex extracts for conditions like Alzheimer's disease.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly testing the *systemic cost* of targeted polyphenol delivery, an underexplored counterpoint to prevailing bioavailability research, by integrating neurobehavioral outcomes with gut microbiome and multi-organ antioxidant/anti-inflammatory analyses.

proposals.title: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Adverse Effects and Long-Term Safety of Rhodiola rosea Extracts: Focus on Bioactive Compounds and Gut Microbiota Interactions
proposals.motivation: Rhodiola rosea is a widely consumed adaptogen for stress and fatigue, but its safety profile remains inadequately synthesized, with conflicting reports on side effects like irritability and insomnia. Given Dr. Daglia's expertise in polyphenol and saponin bioactivity and gut interactions, a rigorous safety analysis is crucial to inform clinical use and regulatory guidelines, especially concerning long-term consumption and potential herb-drug interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic analysis will reveal that adverse effects of Rhodiola rosea are dose-dependent and may be linked to specific bioactive compounds (e.g., rosavins, salidroside) and their modulation of gut microbiota and metabolic pathways.
proposals.method: We will conduct a PRISMA-guided systematic review of clinical trials, observational studies, and case reports (pre-2026). Data on adverse events, dosage, extract composition, and study duration will be extracted. A meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials will be performed where feasible. An in silico analysis (molecular docking) will screen major Rhodiola constituents (e.g., rosavin, kaempferol derivatives) for potential off-target interactions with human proteins, building on Dr. Daglia's recent in silico approaches. The review will specifically analyze reported effects in the context of gut microbiota changes, linking to saponin and polyphenol research.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive safety profile of Rhodiola rosea that integrates clinical data with mechanistic insights into compound-specific effects and gut microbiome interactions. It will establish evidence-based dosing guidelines and identify critical gaps for future toxicological and clinical research on adaptogens.
proposals.novelty: This review moves beyond cataloging side effects by employing a phytochemistry-informed and mechanism-focused approach, explicitly connecting adverse outcomes to specific bioactive compounds and their documented interactions with absorption, metabolism, and the gut ecosystem—a perspective largely absent from existing safety reviews.

proposals.title: Network Pharmacology Elucidation of Rhodiola rosea's Multi-Target Mechanisms in the Brain: Integrating ADME Screening, Target Prediction, and Interactome Analysis
proposals.motivation: Rhodiola rosea is a widely used adaptogen with documented neuroprotective and cognitive-enhancing effects, but its complex phytochemistry, including salidroside, rosavin, and various polyphenols, suggests multi-target actions that are poorly understood. Pre-2026, network pharmacology has emerged as a powerful paradigm for deciphering the 'compound-target-pathway' networks of herbal medicines, offering a systematic alternative to the single-target model. This approach is crucial for validating traditional uses and guiding targeted neuropharmacological development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primary bioactive compounds of Rhodiola rosea will act synergistically on a network of brain-related targets, primarily involved in neuroinflammation, oxidative stress, and neurotrophic signaling pathways, which collectively underpin its adaptogenic and neuroprotective properties.
proposals.method: We will employ a standard network pharmacology workflow. First, ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion) screening will filter R. rosea compounds from databases like TCMSP using OB and DL parameters. Potential targets will be predicted using SwissTargetPrediction and PharmMapper. Brain-specific protein-protein interaction networks will be constructed using STRING and validated targets, followed by KEGG and GO enrichment analysis via DAVID. Key network topology measures (degree, betweenness) will identify hub targets and central pathways. Molecular docking (AutoDock Vina) will validate key compound-target interactions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, systems-level map of R. rosea's action on the brain interactome, identifying key synergistic targets and pathways. It will offer a mechanistic rationale for its cognitive and neuroprotective effects, bridging ethnopharmacology and modern systems biology. The resulting network model will serve as a foundation for future experimental validation and rational formulation design.
proposals.novelty: While network pharmacology has been applied to other herbs, a focused, brain-specific interactome analysis for R. rosea, integrating its full phytochemical profile with pre-2026 computational tools, is novel. This moves beyond describing isolated compound effects to modeling the systemic polypharmacology underlying its adaptogenic claim.

proposals.title: A Prospective Cohort Study on Dietary and Supplemental Rhodiola rosea Intake and the Risk of Neurodegenerative Disease in an Aging Population
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 evidence indicates Rhodiola rosea extracts, rich in polyphenols like salidroside and rosavins, exhibit neuroprotective, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties in preclinical models. However, a critical translational gap exists: there is a lack of population-level epidemiological data on whether habitual Rhodiola consumption is associated with reduced incidence of Alzheimer's disease and related dementias in humans. Establishing such a correlation is essential to justify targeted clinical trials and public health dietary recommendations for cognitive aging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher dietary or supplemental intake of Rhodiola rosea is associated with a lower incidence of neurodegenerative disease diagnoses in an aging population, and that this association is mediated by modulation of systemic inflammatory and metabolic biomarkers.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study nested within an existing large-scale aging population database (e.g., UK Biobank). Rhodiola intake will be quantified at baseline using validated food frequency and dietary supplement questionnaires, creating exposure categories. The primary outcome is incident diagnosis of neurodegenerative disease (ICD-10 codes), identified via linked electronic health records over a 10-year follow-up. We will employ Cox proportional hazards regression models, adjusting for confounders (age, sex, APOE-ε4 status, education, overall diet quality, physical activity). A mediation analysis will assess the role of pre-2026 identified biomarkers (e.g., CRP, BDNF, fasting glucose) using structural equation modeling.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale epidemiological evidence directly linking Rhodiola rosea intake to neurodegenerative disease risk in humans. It will translate promising in vitro and animal model findings (e.g., on scopolamine-induced amnesia) to a population health context. The results will identify potential mediating pathways and inform the design of future randomized controlled trials for cognitive decline prevention.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Daglia's prior work has characterized the bioactive compounds and mechanistic effects of plant extracts in laboratory settings, this proposal innovatively applies her phytochemistry expertise to design and interpret a population-level epidemiological study, a novel methodological scale for her research program. It uniquely focuses on Rhodiola rosea, a widely used but epidemiologically understudied adaptogen, in relation to hard clinical endpoints of neurodegenerative disease.

ideas.description: This research fuses the concepts by engineering a non-plant chassis, such as algae, to overexpress both flavonoid biosynthetic pathways and the human TET1 enzyme via CRISPR activation systems. The engineered algae will produce flavonoid metabolites (e.g., luteolin) within a cellular environment containing elevated TET1, potentially leading to novel, stabilized, or pre-complexed bioactive compounds. This system will allow for the direct comparison of algal-produced, TET1-associated flavonoids against standard luteolin treatment in cancer cell models, testing both production efficiency and therapeutic efficacy. The synergy creates a self-contained epigenetic therapy production and delivery platform that simultaneously addresses optimal biosynthesis and mechanistic activity.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Metabolic Engineering for On-Demand, Tumor-Specific Luteolin Production
ideas.description: This research fuses the exploration of luteolin's anti-cancer mechanism with dynamic metabolic engineering to create a novel cell-based delivery system. Engineered probiotic or patient-derived cells will be equipped with a luteolin biosynthetic pathway under the control of feedback algorithms that respond to tumor microenvironment signals (e.g., hypoxia, lactate). This closed-loop system aims to produce and deliver luteolin locally and on-demand at the tumor site. The approach allows for the direct testing of the Nrf2/p53 demethylation mechanism's efficacy across multiple cancer types (e.g., breast, lung) in physiologically relevant, dynamic conditions while minimizing systemic exposure and metabolic burden on the producer cells.

ideas.title: Disentangling Paracrine Necessity: A Modular 3D Co-culture Platform to Test if Luteolin's Anti-Tumor Effects are Mediated by the Stromal Niche
ideas.description: This project tests the specific hypothesis that luteolin's primary anti-proliferative and pro-apoptotic effects in solid tumors are contingent upon its modulation of paracrine signaling from cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) to cancer cells, rather than direct cell-autonomous action. We employ a modular 3D co-culture system featuring CAFs and tumor cells with inducible genetic perturbations in key candidate pathways (e.g., CXCL12/CXCR4, IL-6/JAK/STAT). By physically separating cell types in transwells, selectively applying luteolin, and performing multiplexed phospho-protein and cytokine secretion analysis, the system can definitively assign whether luteolin's efficacy requires stromal-derived signals. This approach moves beyond correlation to establish causal, mechanistic links between the compound, the tumor microenvironment, and cancer cell fate.

ideas.title: Engineered Microbial Consortia for Targeted Degradation of Chemotherapy Metabolites and Synergistic Drug Potentiation
ideas.description: This research proposes to engineer synthetic microbial consortia to be administered alongside luteolin and standard colon cancer chemotherapies. The consortia will be designed with two complementary functions: first, to locally degrade excess luteolin or its metabolic byproducts in the gut lumen to fine-tune its bioavailability and synergistic window with drugs like 5-FU or oxaliplatin. Second, specific microbial pathways will be engineered to convert chemotherapeutic agents into more active forms or to degrade compounds that cause dose-limiting toxicity. This approach creates a dynamic, living adjunct therapy that actively manages the pharmacokinetic landscape within the tumor microenvironment to maximize epigenetic and pro-apoptotic synergy while protecting healthy tissue.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporally controlled flavonoid sink depletion for precise flux observation
ideas.description: This approach engineers plants with a system for precise, localized reduction of flavonoid production, rather than a whole-plant knockdown. Using tissue-specific and/or chemically inducible CRISPR interference (CRISPRi) targeting early flavonoid biosynthetic genes, flavonoid synthesis is disrupted only in a defined, non-critical cell type. This creates a controlled metabolic void (sink depletion) in that specific tissue compartment, enabling high-resolution tracking of how central carbon precursors are rerouted using isotopic labeling and metabolomics. The system isolates the sink-engineering effect from the pleiotropic consequences of global flavonoid deficiency, yielding clearer causal data on metabolic network flexibility.

ideas.title: Optimize luteolin delivery and validate target exposure prior to efficacy testing in colon cancer models
ideas.description: This redesigned study first addresses the critical pharmacokinetic limitations of luteolin by developing and validating an optimized formulation to achieve sustained, therapeutically relevant plasma and tumor tissue concentrations in murine models. Using this validated dosing regimen, the research then employs a comprehensive PK/PD approach, correlating luteolin and metabolite levels with direct biomarkers of the proposed mechanisms (e.g., caspase cleavage, global/DNA methyltransferase activity) in tumor tissue. Only after confirming target engagement and modulation in vivo does the protocol proceed to a longitudinal efficacy study, assessing tumor growth and survival. This sequential design ensures any negative efficacy outcome is interpretable and not an artifact of poor drug delivery.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Single-Cell Microfluidic Profiling of Nrf2-Dependent Flavonoid Adaptation in Cancer
ideas.description: This research combines droplet microfluidics with molecular oncology to investigate the context-dependent role of Nrf2 in cancer. We will encapsulate individual cancer cells, expose them to luteolin and other stressors, and use fluorescent biosensors or secreted flavonoid assays within droplets to simultaneously track Nrf2 activation and flavonoid production at single-cell resolution over time. This allows for the large-scale screening of how tumor cell subpopulations leverage flavonoid-associated Nrf2 upregulation for adaptive survival, potentially identifying specific metabolic conditions or tumor stages where this pathway switches from protective to detrimental. The platform moves beyond population averages to uncover heterogeneous, dynamic cell states that could explain therapeutic resistance and inform timing for Nrf2-targeting interventions.

ideas.title: Adapting Antiviral Broad-Spectrum Screening to Test Luteolin's Nrf2/p53 Mechanism Across Diverse Cancer Lineages
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from virology's approach to finding broad-spectrum antivirals, this research adapts the strategy of high-throughput, multi-target phenotypic screening. In virology, compounds targeting a common host factor are screened across a diverse panel of viruses to identify broad-spectrum activity. Adapted here, the Nrf2/p53 demethylation mechanism induced by luteolin is treated as a 'host-targeted' anticancer strategy. The research will involve systematically screening luteolin and its synthetic analogs against a curated panel of cancer cell lines from multiple lineages (e.g., breast, lung, pancreatic, glioblastoma) using standardized, high-content assays measuring Nrf2 pathway activation, specific p53 demethylation marks, and resultant cell death. This parallel, systematic approach will efficiently determine the range of cancers vulnerable to this mechanism, identify resistant lineages for mechanistic follow-up, and potentially reveal lineage-specific co-factors required for efficacy, moving beyond piecemeal investigation of single cancer types.

ideas.title: Adapting Synergistic Pest Control Strategies to Test Luteolin's Chemotherapeutic Enhancement
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Integrated Pest Management in agriculture, where a sub-lethal botanical stressor is used to sensitize pests to a primary synthetic insecticide, this research adapts that synergistic strategy. The study will test if pre-treating colon cancer cells with a low dose of luteolin—to epigenetically 'prime' them via its known mechanisms—enhances the efficacy of subsequently applied, reduced doses of standard chemotherapeutics like 5-FU or oxaliplatin. The core investigation will measure whether this sequential, sensitization-focused combination strategy yields greater apoptotic response and growth inhibition compared to concurrent administration or either agent alone, aiming to establish a protocol that could maximize therapeutic synergy while minimizing chemotherapy-related toxicity.

ideas.title: Predictive Ecosystem-Aware Engineering of Flavonoids (PEA-Flav)
ideas.description: This research direction integrates machine learning for pathway prediction with ecosystem-level impact assessment to enable the rational design of 'ecosystem-compatible' flavonoids. Machine learning models will be trained on multi-omics datasets—combining plant enzyme sequences, resulting flavonoid chemical structures, and longitudinal soil microbiome profiles—to learn the complex relationships between genetic parts, metabolite output, and soil community outcomes. The core synergy is the development of predictive models that can recommend specific enzyme variants or synthetic pathways to produce novel flavonoids optimized for both agricultural traits (e.g., stress resistance) and predefined, beneficial soil microbiome states (e.g., enhanced nitrogen cycling). This moves beyond simple production or observation to the proactive, ecosystem-aware engineering of plant metabolites for sustainable agriculture.

ideas.title: Metabolite-Centric Pathway Reconstruction via Comparative Genomics
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from computational linguistics and cryptography to the inverse problem of flavonoid pathway discovery. The core analogy views novel, unknown flavonoid structures as ciphertexts or undeciphered scripts. The analogous solution—using comparative analysis against known systems to reverse-engineer underlying rules—is adapted by employing large-scale comparative plant genomics and metabolomics as the reference corpus. The research idea involves developing computational frameworks that treat flavonoid structural motifs as decodable units. By performing pattern mining across diverse plant genomes and metabolomes, the framework will correlate these structural motifs with specific genomic features (e.g., co-localized genes, promoter elements, phylogenetic distribution of enzyme families). This process aims to reverse-engineer the biosynthetic logic, predicting novel enzymes and reconstructing previously unknown pathways directly from the flavonoid 'output,' scaling discovery in both metabolite and genomic space.

ideas.title: Map luteolin's emergent network effects in colon cancer using in vivo multiplexed pathway analysis
ideas.description: This study investigates luteolin's anti-colon cancer effects in animal models by abandoning the assumption of a hierarchical, linear pathway. Instead, it employs high-throughput, multiplexed analyses (e.g., phospho-proteomics, single-cell RNA-seq, DNA methylome) on treated tumors to capture simultaneous, system-wide changes across apoptosis, epigenetic, metabolic, and immune-modulatory nodes. The goal is to construct and validate a dynamic, decentralized network model of flavonoid action, identifying critical, context-sensitive network motifs or 'pressure points' that correlate with therapeutic efficacy, rather than a single master regulator.

ideas.title: Sequential, multi-mechanism demethylation blockade to dissect TET-independent apoptotic triggers of luteolin
ideas.description: This study employs a temporally controlled, triple-layer blockade of DNA demethylation to rigorously test if luteolin's pro-apoptotic effects are TET/demethylation-independent. Cells are first exposed to luteolin to initiate its cellular effects, then subjected to a sequential and inducible combination of genetic (CRISPRi knockdown of TET1/2/3), pharmacological (TET catalytic inhibitor), and metabolic (α-KG depletion) interventions to achieve a near-total and verifiable shutdown of active DNA demethylation. Apoptosis is tracked throughout, and the functional success of the blockade is quantitatively confirmed via bisulfite sequencing, ensuring that any residual apoptotic signal is definitively uncoupled from canonical demethylation activity.

ideas.title: Multi-omic mapping of pollinator-optimized flavonoid landscapes
ideas.description: This research integrates transcriptomic, proteomic, and metabolomic data to deconstruct the complete biosynthetic and regulatory network of flavonoids, but with a novel, applied objective: to identify the distinct multi-omic 'signatures' associated with pollinator-attraction profiles versus pathogen-defense profiles. By comparing these signatures across plant species and tissues, we can pinpoint the key regulatory nodes and enzyme isoforms that favor the production of attraction-specific flavonoid blends. This knowledge enables the reverse-engineering of pathways to optimize floral displays for pollinator vision and preference, moving beyond static pathway description to the dynamic design of crops and ecological restorations with enhanced pollination services.

ideas.title: Apply CRISPRa with synthetic feedback control to titrate TET1 expression
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from synthetic biology and metabolic engineering, where synthetic feedback control circuits are used to dynamically titrate gene expression in response to metabolic state. The analogous solution involves using metabolite-sensing promoters or riboswitches to optimize pathway flux. Adapted to the original research context, this involves engineering a CRISPR activation (CRISPRa) system that is not constitutive but is instead placed under the control of a synthetic circuit responsive to a cellular readout of DNA methylation (e.g., 5hmC levels). This system would dynamically upregulate endogenous TET1 expression only as needed to shift the methylation landscape, allowing for precise titration and a more physiologically relevant comparison to the dose-dependent pharmacological action of luteolin in cancer cells.

ideas.title: Context-Driven Chassis Selection via Multi-Criteria Benchmarking for Flavonoid Bioproduction
ideas.description: This project establishes a framework to rationally select the optimal bioproduction chassis for flavonoids by moving beyond a single 'efficiency' metric. We define a panel of high-value target flavonoids and a comprehensive set of performance vectors encompassing biological performance, engineering tractability, economic scalability, and sustainability. A standardized experimental pipeline is used to benchmark diverse non-plant and plant chassis against all vectors for each target compound. The resulting data feeds into a decision-support model that identifies the most robust chassis for a given product and production context, transforming chassis selection from a generic comparison to a targeted, application-specific design choice.

ideas.title: Adapting Redundancy Bypass from Fault-Tolerant Computing to p53-Deficient Cancer Therapy
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'redundancy bypass' principle from fault-tolerant computing to challenge the assumption that p53 is always functional for luteolin's efficacy. In systems engineering, when a primary component fails, solutions are designed to activate alternative, parallel pathways to maintain core function. Adapted to this research context, we treat mutant/inactive p53 as a 'failed node' and systematically test whether luteolin's efficacy in colon cancer cells relies on its ability to map onto and activate latent, compensatory tumor-suppressive pathways (e.g., via p73, p63, or other p53-independent death pathways), thereby bypassing the need for the p53-Nrf2 interaction entirely. This re-frames the drug's mechanism as exploiting inherent redundancy in the cellular signaling network.

ideas.title: Establish causal necessity of TET family members in luteolin's acute epigenetic and apoptotic effects via simultaneous, inducible perturbation
ideas.description: This study will determine if TET enzymes are necessary and direct mediators of luteolin's action by implementing a system of acute, simultaneous intervention. Using cell lines with doxycycline-inducible CRISPR/Cas9 for rapid, combinatorial knockout of TET1, TET2, and TET3, we will treat cells with luteolin immediately following gene ablation. Primary outcomes will be measured in real-time using a CRISPR-dCas9-Tet1cd epigenetic reporter targeted to the endogenous Nrf2 promoter to monitor locus-specific demethylation dynamics, coupled with live-cell imaging for apoptosis. This design isolates the direct, causal role of each TET protein by preventing compensatory mechanisms and separating primary epigenetic effects from downstream cellular consequences.

ideas.title: Develop burden-aware, sensor-integrated genetic circuits for robust and stable flavonoid production
ideas.description: This redesigned approach engineers genetic circuits that not only provide inducible control but also autonomously manage the metabolic consequences of flavonoid production. It incorporates real-time biosensors for key pathway intermediates and cellular burden markers, coupled to feedback loops that modulate enzyme expression and resource allocation. This creates a self-regulating system that maintains host cell health, prevents toxic intermediate accumulation, and ensures stable, high-yield production over extended cultivation periods, directly addressing the instability inherent in simpler static circuits.

ideas.title: Distributed Biosynthesis via Metabolic Handoff
ideas.description: This idea challenges the necessity of full pathway reconstitution in a single host by adapting a solution from distributed computing. In that field, complex tasks are broken into subtasks processed across specialized nodes with handoff protocols. The adapted research idea proposes a synthetic microbial co-culture system for flavonoid production. One host ('generator') is engineered to produce and export key, unstable flavonoid precursors. A second, specialized host ('finisher') imports these intermediates to efficiently complete the synthesis and handle final product storage or export. This 'metabolic handoff' approach distributes the biosynthetic burden, potentially overcoming toxicity, regulatory, or efficiency bottlenecks inherent to constructing the entire pathway in one organism.

ideas.title: A Dynamic Bayesian Network Model for Multi-Omics Integration in Flavonoid Pathway Analysis
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from systems ecology—the use of Dynamic Bayesian Networks (DBNs) to integrate noisy, multi-stream, time-series data for predicting ecosystem states—to the problem of multi-omics integration in plant biochemistry. The analogous problem in ecology involves synthesizing climate, population, and geochemical data to understand complex, lagged regulatory interactions leading to collapse. The adapted idea applies this DBN framework to transcriptomic, proteomic, and metabolomic time-series data from plants under stress or developmental cues. This will create a probabilistic model that infers the causal, time-delayed regulatory logic of the flavonoid pathway, identifying key hidden regulatory nodes and predicting metabolic outcomes from genetic or environmental perturbations, moving beyond simple correlation to a dynamic, predictive understanding of multi-level regulation.

ideas.title: Employ droplet microfluidics with integrated FRET-based biosensors for single-cell flavonoid screening
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from immunology and cancer research, where droplet microfluidics has been successfully paired with FRET-based biosensors to screen single cells for specific intracellular biochemical activities. The analogous solution involves encapsulating cells in picoliter droplets alongside lysis components and a FRET sensor that reacts to a target molecule upon cell rupture. Adapted to plant research, the method would employ microfluidic devices to generate droplets containing individual plant protoplasts, a lysis agent, and a FRET biosensor engineered to detect a core flavonoid pathway metabolite. Upon lysis, the flavonoid interacts with the sensor, causing a quantifiable change in FRET efficiency, allowing for the high-throughput screening and sorting of thousands of plant cells based on their precise flavonoid production levels at single-cell resolution.

ideas.title: Applying the 'Non-Model Organism' Paradigm from Genetics to Metabolic Chassis Selection
ideas.description: This idea applies the 'non-model organism' paradigm from comparative genetics to metabolic engineering. In genetics, moving beyond a narrow set of model organisms revealed unique biological capabilities. Similarly, we challenge the assumption that traditional plant platforms are optimal for flavonoid production by proposing a systematic, comparative analysis of diverse non-plant photosynthetic chassis like microalgae and cyanobacteria. By adapting the genetic field's solution—broad phylogenetic screening to discover pre-adapted hosts—we aim to identify chassis with inherently superior flavonoid production efficiency due to their native metabolic networks and regulatory architectures.

ideas.title: Closed-loop biosensor-guided CRISPRi/a for adaptive flavonoid pathway control
ideas.description: This system overcomes static control by implementing real-time, feedback-driven modulation of flavonoid biosynthesis. It combines CRISPR interference/activation (CRISPRi/a) with genetically encoded biosensors that monitor key metabolic nodes or cellular stress. A computational controller processes this live fluorescence data to calculate and deliver precise optogenetic or small-molecule inputs that tune dCas9-gRNA expression levels. This creates a dynamic equilibrium, automatically adjusting pathway enzyme expression to maximize flux toward the desired flavonoid while minimizing metabolic burden and toxic intermediate accumulation.

ideas.title: Integrated Directed Evolution of Flavonoid Pathways Using In Vivo Biosensor-Coupled Fitness Selection
ideas.description: This approach applies directed evolution not to isolated enzymes, but within the context of the complete flavonoid pathway in a living microbial host. A genetically encoded biosensor for a toxic or regulatory pathway intermediate is used to couple cellular fitness directly to balanced flux. High-throughput mutant libraries are then screened under selective pressure where survival and growth correlate with optimal enzyme performance that avoids metabolic bottlenecks. This ensures evolved variants improve overall pathway efficiency and final product yield in a production-ready chassis.

ideas.title: Model flavonoid regulation as a resilient, decentralized network using ecological stability theory
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecology, where the paradigm shifted from hierarchical food chains to decentralized interaction networks to explain ecosystem stability. The analogous solution involves modeling ecosystems as networks where properties like modularity, connectance, and functional redundancy determine resilience, moving beyond the search for single keystone controllers. Adapted to flavonoid regulation, this means constructing and analyzing a quantitative interaction network of all known pathway components (e.g., MYB, bHLH, WD40 proteins, enzymes, metabolites). The research would use computational tools from systems ecology to measure network topology and simulate perturbations, testing if flavonoid accumulation patterns are best predicted by emergent network properties rather than the activity of any presumed hierarchical master regulator. This directly challenges the assumption of hierarchical control by offering a formal, testable framework for decentralized regulation.

ideas.title: Infer and validate causal regulatory logic in flavonoid biosynthesis using predictive network engineering
ideas.description: This project moves beyond descriptive network mapping to establish causal, predictive models of flavonoid regulation. It employs systematic, combinatorial perturbations of transcription factors and chromatin regulators in plant cells, followed by single-cell multi-omics profiling to build a logic-gate style model of regulatory interactions. The core validation involves designing synthetic promoters based on the inferred logic, introducing them into plants, and quantitatively testing the model's predictions of flavonoid accumulation patterns in response to defined genetic and environmental perturbations.

ideas.description: This study employs a multi-omics, time-resolved framework to definitively identify promoter demethylation events that are direct, functional consequences of luteolin exposure. Following luteolin treatment of cancer cells, time-course whole-genome bisulfite sequencing is paired with global run-on sequencing (GRO-seq) to capture nascent transcription, isolating methylation changes that precede and correlate with immediate transcriptional upregulation. Candidate causal genes are then validated using CRISPRi to test if their silencing abrogates specific anti-proliferative or pro-apoptotic effects of luteolin, moving beyond correlation to establish a functional chain of causality from compound to epigenetic change to phenotypic outcome.

ideas.title: Scaling Flavonoid Production via Engineered Plant Surface Microbiomes
ideas.description: This idea expands on using root hairs or trichomes as scalable sites for flavonoid production by drawing an analogy from marine biotechnology's approach to coral reef symbiosis. In corals, productivity is scaled by engineering the symbiotic microalgae within the host. Adapted to plant systems, the solution is to engineer the native microbial symbionts (bacteria or fungi) that colonize the surfaces of root hairs or trichomes. These engineered microbes would be designed to efficiently convert plant-derived precursors into target flavonoids and secrete them, transforming the plant's extensive surface area into a vast, productive bioreactor. This symbiotic scaling strategy leverages the plant's architecture and vascular system while outsourcing intensive metabolic production to optimized microbial partners, enabling high-yield flavonoid production without proportionally increasing plant biomass.

ideas.title: Isogenic CRISPRa/i and Inducible Overexpression Triangulation to Define TET1-Specific Phenotypes in Cancer
ideas.description: This study employs a multi-faceted genetic toolkit to precisely define the phenotypic consequences of modulating TET1 expression, eliminating confounding factors from small molecules. In an isogenic cancer cell background, we create three parallel models: CRISPRa for TET1 upregulation, CRISPRi for its knockdown, and a dCas9-only control. Alongside, we generate a doxycycline-inducible TET1 overexpression system for orthogonal, dose- and time-controlled validation. Phenotypic comparisons across these models—assessing DNA hydroxymethylation, gene expression, proliferation, and invasion—will rigorously attribute effects specifically to TET1 levels, not to off-target drug actions or genetic tool artifacts.

ideas.title: Synchronized Co-culture System with Sensor-Triggered Intermediate Transfer for Robust Split-Pathway Synthesis
ideas.description: This redesigned approach establishes a syntrophic co-culture where a precursor-producing strain and a finishing strain are co-optimized to grow in a shared bioreactor environment. The precursor producer is engineered with a biosensor that monitors the intracellular accumulation of the key flavonoid intermediate; upon reaching an optimal threshold, this sensor triggers either a controlled lysis circuit or the expression of specific export pumps. Simultaneously, the finishing strain's uptake and conversion pathways are induced, creating a synchronized 'just-in-time' transfer that minimizes intermediate exposure to the extracellular medium. This closed, single-vessel system eliminates transfer logistics, stabilizes the labile intermediate, and maximizes carbon flux toward the final product through temporal and population-level coordination.

ideas.title: Predictive cybergenetic control of metabolic pathways using model-based state estimation and intermittent sensing
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the critical bottleneck of real-time metabolite sensing by implementing a model-predictive control (MPC) strategy for dynamic metabolic engineering. An ensemble of mechanistic and data-driven models continuously predicts intracellular fluxes and metabolite levels, with these predictions periodically corrected by experimentally feasible, low-burden biosensor measurements. The controller uses these updated state estimates to compute optimal actuator inputs, such as light intensity for optogenetic systems or inducer levels for orthogonal regulators, to stabilize fluxes and maximize productivity. This creates a robust, closed-loop system that achieves dynamic control despite the inherent limitations and noise of biological sensing.

ideas.title: Adaptive Cruise Control for Metabolic Pathways
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) systems in automotive engineering to the problem of stabilizing flavonoid pathway fluxes. In ACC, radar sensors feed distance data to a controller that dynamically adjusts throttle/braking actuators to maintain a set gap. Analogously, we propose engineering a synthetic biological control loop: a genetically-encoded biosensor acts as the 'radar' to monitor a key flavonoid pathway intermediate. This signal is processed by a synthetic genetic circuit (the 'controller') that compares it to a setpoint. The controller then modulates 'actuator' promoters driving expression of bottleneck enzymes, dynamically tuning their levels to maintain a desired, stable flux output against internal metabolic noise and external feedstock variations.

ideas.title: Ecosystem Flux Analysis of Engineered Flavonoid Impacts
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from oceanography, where the impact of phytoplankton blooms on marine carbon cycles is studied using integrated remote sensing and in-situ sensor arrays. The analogous solution combines satellite data to track bloom dynamics with autonomous sensors measuring real-time water chemistry and microbial activity, linking local events to ecosystem fluxes. Adapted to the agricultural context, the research will deploy a network of soil sensors around engineered flavonoid-producing crops to monitor real-time chemical and biological changes in the rhizosphere. This dense, local data will be integrated with drone- and satellite-based remote sensing of crop health and field conditions. The synthesis will create a predictive spatiotemporal model to quantify how engineered flavonoid production scales its impact, altering soil carbon/nutrient cycling and microbiome assembly from the root interface to the entire field ecosystem.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Tomography for High-Resolution Luteolin-Induced Epigenomic Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from geophysical seismic tomography to epigenomics. In seismology, waves from multiple earthquakes are used to computationally reconstruct a 3D image of the Earth's interior. Analogously, we propose treating sequential, low-dose luteolin treatments as generating 'epigenetic perturbation waves' in cancer cells. By performing whole-genome bisulfite sequencing at multiple time points after each treatment (acting as 'recording stations'), we can collect a rich, multi-source dataset. Applying computational inversion models to this data will allow us to reconstruct a dynamic, high-resolution spatiotemporal map of genome-wide demethylation. This approach moves beyond a static list of demethylated promoters to reveal the primary epigenetic loci targeted by luteolin and the subsequent cascading waves of demethylation across the genome, identifying core regulatory networks and sequence-dependent propagation patterns.
